features: Full feature set with no paywall

enrichment.short_description: Directus is an open-source, self-hostable headless CMS and backend that connects to any SQL database and instantly generates REST and GraphQL APIs with a no-code admin Studio.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (free self-hosted core, paid cloud/team/enterprise tiers)
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 55 employees across North America, Europe, and Asia
enrichment.seo_title: Directus Review: Open-Source Headless CMS & Backend
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Directus turns any SQL database into a headless CMS with instant REST/GraphQL APIs and a no-code admin panel. Compare pricing, features, pros, and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Directus is an open-source, self-hostable data platform that functions as a collaborative backend and headless content management system, sitting directly on top of an existing SQL database and instantly exposing that data through REST and GraphQL APIs alongside a visual, no-code admin interface called the Studio. Rather than requiring developers to design a proprietary schema inside a vendor-controlled system, Directus connects to a database the developer already owns or creates - including PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, SQLite, and other SQL-compatible engines - and introspects its structure to automatically generate CRUD APIs, an authentication and role-based access control layer, and a customizable admin panel for managing content, without locking the underlying data into a proprietary format.

The Directus project has an unusually long history for a modern headless CMS. Its origins trace back to 2004, when Ben Haynes built an early, Flash-based database management tool that would eventually evolve into Directus. The product was substantially rebuilt from the ground up in 2019 as Directus 6/7, transforming it into the modern, JavaScript- and Node.js-based open-source headless CMS that the market recognizes today; this rebuild is credited largely to Rijk van Zanten, who partnered with Haynes to modernize the framework - originally to better support his own master's program work - and later became the project's CTO. The company behind Directus operates under the legal entity Monospace Inc., headquartered in Brooklyn, New York, and as of early 2026 employs approximately 55 people distributed across North America, Europe, and Asia. Directus has raised outside capital, including a Series A round of 7 million dollars led by True Ventures in November 2022, funding that has gone toward building out the commercial cloud-hosting and enterprise side of the business alongside continued open-source development.

Architecturally, Directus is distinctive among headless CMS platforms because it is 'database-first' rather than 'schema-first': most competing headless CMS products (Contentful, Sanity, Strapi in its default mode) require content models to be defined inside the CMS itself, which then owns and stores the data, whereas Directus instead wraps around a database the user already controls, meaning teams can adopt Directus on top of an existing production database without a data migration, and can drop Directus entirely later while keeping their data intact in a standard SQL format. On top of this data layer, Directus adds Flows, a visual workflow-automation and business-logic builder for triggering actions (webhooks, emails, data transformations) based on events; a built-in AI assistant for authoring and querying content; and, more recently, a native Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that allows AI agents to interact with a Directus-managed dataset as a structured tool, reflecting the product's expansion toward AI-agent-compatible backend infrastructure.

Licensing for Directus is handled under the Monospace Sustainable Core License (MSCL) 1.0, a source-available license derived from the Fair Core License model, rather than a fully permissive open-source license like MIT or Apache 2.0. In practice, this license permits free self-hosting and commercial use for the vast majority of users, but the company reserves certain terms designed to prevent large competitors from repackaging and reselling Directus as a competing hosted service; organizations under roughly 5 million dollars in total annual income qualify for what Directus calls its Open Innovation Grant, giving them free self-hosting rights or discounted cloud hosting regardless of company size restrictions tied to seats. This licensing model - source-available with commercial protections rather than pure open source - mirrors a broader industry trend among infrastructure software companies (similar to licenses used by MongoDB, Elastic, and HashiCorp in past years) seeking to balance open distribution with a sustainable commercial business.

On pricing, Directus offers four tiers. The Core plan is free and includes 3 user seats, 25 collections, 5 automation flows, the AI assistant, and advanced role-based access control, suitable for evaluating the product or powering small individual projects, with self-hosting always free and cloud hosting available as a 99-dollar-per-month add-on. The Team plan costs 499 dollars per month billed annually (or 599 dollars per month billed monthly) and includes 10 SSO seats, 50 collections (expandable to 100), 20 flows, SAML/OIDC single sign-on, and included cloud hosting, aimed at teams needing a supported, production-ready deployment. The Enterprise plan is custom-priced and adds unlimited flows, 200-plus collections, custom large-language-model integration, offline mode, AI-powered translations, telemetry opt-out, and premium support for organizations with complex compliance or scale requirements. Separately, the Open Innovation Grant offers free self-hosted access, or 99 dollars per month for cloud hosting, specifically for smaller organizations under the revenue and headcount thresholds described above.

Directus's key differentiators are its database-first, non-proprietary data model; its dual REST-and-GraphQL API generation out of the box; its no-code Studio admin panel that non-developers can use to manage content without touching code; and its growing set of AI-native features, including the MCP server for agentic workflows. Its target users span solo developers and agencies building custom client backends, startups needing a fast admin panel over their production database, and enterprises building internal tools or data platforms who want to avoid vendor lock-in. Its most direct competitors are Strapi, Payload CMS, and, in the schema-first camp, Contentful, Sanity, and Hygraph, along with backend-as-a-service platforms like Supabase and Hasura that likewise emphasize instant APIs over a SQL database; Directus differentiates from most of these by supporting a much broader range of existing SQL database engines rather than requiring a specific proprietary or PostgreSQL-only backend. Notable facts include Directus's status as one of the most widely starred open-source headless CMS projects on GitHub, its adoption across government, media, and enterprise data-platform use cases, and its 20-plus-year lineage as one of the longest-running continuously developed CMS codebases in the industry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Directus is an open-source, self-hostable data platform that turns any existing SQL database into a headless CMS, instantly generating REST and GraphQL APIs plus a no-code admin Studio for managing content, users, and permissions.

Originally rooted in a Flash-based database tool from 2004, Directus was rebuilt from the ground up in 2019 into the modern, database-first headless CMS now maintained by Monospace Inc., a company headquartered in Brooklyn, New York.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Directus connects directly to PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, SQLite, and other SQL databases without requiring a data migration, and layers on role-based access control, Flows for workflow automation, an AI assistant, and a native MCP server for AI agent integration.

Because it wraps around a database a team already controls rather than owning a proprietary data store, organizations can adopt or remove Directus without losing or reformatting their underlying data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Directus offers a free self-hosted Core plan (3 seats, 25 collections, 5 flows), a Team plan at $499-$599/month with SSO and included cloud hosting, custom Enterprise pricing, and a free or discounted Open Innovation Grant tier for organizations under $5M in annual revenue.

features.title: Database-first architecture
features.description: Connects to an existing SQL database and generates APIs without requiring a proprietary schema or data migration.

features.title: Instant REST and GraphQL APIs
features.description: Automatically exposes CRUD endpoints in both REST and GraphQL based on the connected database's structure.

features.title: No-code admin Studio
features.description: A visual interface for managing content, collections, and users without writing code.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Granular permissions system for controlling who can read, write, or manage specific data and fields.

features.title: Flows automation builder
features.description: A visual workflow engine for triggering webhooks, emails, and data transformations based on events.

features.title: AI assistant and MCP server
features.description: Built-in AI tooling for content authoring plus a native Model Context Protocol server for AI agent integration.

features.title: Self-hosting or managed cloud
features.description: Deploy on your own infrastructure for free or use Directus's managed cloud hosting on paid plans.

features.title: SSO support
features.description: SAML and OIDC single sign-on available on Team and Enterprise plans.

pros: Free self-hosting with no proprietary data lock-in
Works over an existing SQL database rather than requiring a migration
Generates both REST and GraphQL APIs automatically
No-code admin panel usable by non-developers alongside a full API for developers
cons: Licensed under a source-available license (MSCL) rather than a fully permissive open-source license
Cloud and Team pricing ($499+/month) is significant for smaller teams needing hosted support
Collection and flow limits on lower tiers can require an upgrade as projects grow
Learning curve for teams unfamiliar with database-first (versus schema-first) CMS concepts

faqs.question: Is Directus free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Core plan is free for self-hosting with 3 seats, 25 collections, and 5 flows; organizations under $5M in revenue may also qualify for the free or discounted Open Innovation Grant.

faqs.question: What databases does Directus support?
faqs.answer: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, SQLite, and other SQL-compatible database engines.

faqs.question: Is Directus open source?
faqs.answer: Directus is source-available under the Monospace Sustainable Core License (MSCL) 1.0, which permits free self-hosting and commercial use for most organizations.

faqs.question: Who owns Directus?
faqs.answer: Directus is developed and maintained by Monospace Inc., headquartered in Brooklyn, New York.

faqs.question: Does Directus support GraphQL as well as REST?
faqs.answer: Yes, Directus automatically generates both REST and GraphQL APIs from the connected database schema.

faqs.question: What is Directus's Open Innovation Grant?
faqs.answer: A program offering free self-hosted access or discounted cloud hosting to organizations under roughly $5 million in annual revenue.

use_cases.title: Custom backend for web and mobile apps
use_cases.description: Developers use Directus to add instant APIs and an admin panel on top of a production database.

use_cases.title: Enterprise data platform and internal tools
use_cases.description: Organizations use Directus to build internal admin panels and data management tools without vendor lock-in.

use_cases.title: Agency client CMS projects
use_cases.description: Agencies deploy Directus as a flexible, white-label headless CMS for client websites and apps.

features: For organizations under $5M annual revenue and under 50 employees
Full feature access with no artificial limits

enrichment.short_description: Disciple (formerly Disciple Media) is a UK-based platform for building fully branded iOS, Android, and web community apps with courses, livestreams, and monetization, no coding needed.
enrichment.seo_title: Disciple Review: Branded Community App Builder & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Disciple lets creators and brands build their own branded iOS/Android community app with courses, livestreams, and monetization. Compare pricing and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Disciple, formerly marketed as Disciple Media and now operating primarily under the disciple.community brand, is a London-based software platform that lets creators, coaches, membership businesses, and brands build their own fully white-labeled community app for iOS, Android, and web, without writing code. Rather than directing an audience to a third-party social network or a generic community forum, Disciple lets an organization put its own name, logo, and color scheme on an app that appears in the Apple App Store and Google Play Store as an independent product, while Disciple's platform powers the underlying infrastructure: member management, group discussions, direct messaging, live and on-demand video, online courses, events, and payment collection.

The company was founded in the early 2010s - most commonly cited as 2013 - by Benji Vaughan, a musician-turned-technology-entrepreneur, together with co-founder Jeremy Smith. Vaughan's original motivation came from recognizing that musicians and other independent creators lacked a dedicated space to connect directly with their fan communities outside of mainstream social media platforms controlled by algorithms and advertising, rather than by the creator or brand itself. Disciple's early business model operated more like a bespoke app-development agency, building custom-branded apps for musicians and artists on a project basis; the company transitioned from that agency model into a scalable, self-service SaaS business starting around 2016, packaging its app-building capability into repeatable, configurable plans that any creator or organization could subscribe to without a custom development engagement. The company is headquartered in London, England, and, as of recent company-profile data, employs roughly 35 to 50 people distributed across multiple continents including Europe, Asia, and North America.

Product-wise, Disciple's app builder centers on letting an organization configure a community space that looks and feels entirely like their own product. Admins can create multiple discussion groups to segment conversations by topic, cohort, or membership tier; host live and recorded video events and livestreams, including multi-host livestreams on higher tiers; run online courses with monetization options, including one-off purchases or recurring paid access; enable direct messaging between members; apply advanced moderation and admin controls; and pull usage and engagement analytics to understand community health. Every plan includes the organization's own branded native iOS and Android app plus a web version, and Disciple explicitly markets a '0% transaction fee' policy across its plans, meaning that unlike some competing platforms, Disciple does not take a cut of member subscription or course revenue processed through the platform - customers keep 100% of what they charge their members, subject to standard payment-processor fees.

Disciple's pricing is structured as four subscription tiers. The entry-level Branded App plan costs 399 dollars per month on annual billing (469 dollars per month on quarterly billing) and includes unlimited members, two admin seats, five groups, one non-monetizable course, and 5,000 minutes of video. The Branded App Plus plan, described by the company as its most popular tier, costs 599 dollars per month annually (699 dollars per month quarterly) and adds white-labeling with no Disciple branding, five admin seats, ten groups, one monetizable course, 10,000 video minutes, a custom domain, advanced analytics, and Zapier integration. The Branded App Pro plan, at 999 dollars per month annually (1,169 dollars per month quarterly), removes the admin-seat and group caps entirely, allows unlimited monetized courses, provides 20,000 video minutes, adds API access, supports multi-host livestreams, and enables multi-tier member pricing for organizations running several membership levels simultaneously. Above that sits a custom-priced Organisation tier for large enterprises, which includes bespoke feature development, dedicated strategy sessions, migration support from other platforms, and additional security and compliance options; published prices exclude applicable sales tax, and features marked 'unlimited' are subject to fair-usage policies.

Disciple's key differentiator versus generic social platforms or forum software is that the end product is a fully independent, brandable, app-store-listed mobile application rather than a group or page hosted inside someone else's platform, giving creators and brands full ownership of the user experience, their own app-store presence, and direct control over monetization without an intermediary algorithm deciding what content members see. Its most direct competitors in the branded-community-app space are Mighty Networks, Circle, Kajabi, Podia, and Skool, each offering some combination of community, courses, and monetization tools, though Disciple differentiates itself specifically through native, fully white-labeled iOS and Android apps (rather than a web-only or lightly-branded mobile experience) and its 0% transaction fee policy across all tiers. According to the company, Disciple has supported several hundred communities and multiple millions of end users cumulatively across the platform, spanning creator, coaching, wellness, faith, and brand-community use cases. Notable facts include the company's origin story rooted in the music industry, its pivot from a custom-app agency to a repeatable SaaS product around 2016, and its rebrand from 'Disciple Media' to simply 'Disciple' alongside the disciple.community domain as its primary current web presence.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Disciple, formerly Disciple Media, is a London-based platform that lets creators, coaches, and brands build a fully white-labeled community app for iOS, Android, and web, complete with groups, courses, livestreams, and monetization, without writing code.

Founded around 2013 by Benji Vaughan and Jeremy Smith out of a desire to give musicians a direct, algorithm-free way to connect with fans, the company shifted from a bespoke app-development agency into a self-service SaaS platform starting around 2016.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every Disciple plan includes a fully branded native iOS and Android app plus a web version, with tools for discussion groups, direct messaging, live and on-demand video, monetizable online courses, and event hosting.
Disciple applies a 0% transaction fee policy across all plans, meaning customers keep the revenue they collect from members and courses, subject only to standard payment-processing fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Disciple's plans range from Branded App at $399/month (annual billing) up to Branded App Pro at $999/month, with a custom-priced Organisation tier for large enterprises; higher tiers add more admin seats, groups, monetizable courses, video minutes, API access, and multi-host livestreams.

features.title: Fully branded native apps
features.description: Launch your own iOS and Android app in the App Store and Google Play under your own brand, not Disciple's.

features.title: Discussion groups
features.description: Create multiple groups to segment community conversations by topic, tier, or cohort.

features.title: Live and on-demand video
features.description: Host livestreams and video events, including multi-host livestreams on higher-tier plans.

features.title: Monetizable courses
features.description: Build and sell online courses directly within the branded community app.

features.title: 0% transaction fees
features.description: Disciple does not take a cut of member subscription or course revenue processed on the platform.

features.title: Direct messaging
features.description: Members can message each other directly within the branded app.

features.title: Advanced analytics
features.description: Track community engagement and membership metrics on Plus and Pro tiers.

features.title: API access and Zapier integration
features.description: Connect Disciple to other tools and automate workflows on higher-tier plans.

pros: Fully white-labeled, independently app-store-listed iOS and Android apps
0% transaction fees on member and course revenue across all plans
Combines community, courses, and livestreaming in a single platform
No coding required to launch and configure the app
cons: Entry pricing starts at $399/month, higher than many community-platform alternatives
Admin seats, groups, and video minutes are capped on lower tiers
Monetizable courses require the Plus tier or above
Custom Organisation-tier pricing is not publicly listed, requiring a sales conversation

faqs.question: How much does Disciple cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at $399/month billed annually (Branded App) and go up to $999/month (Branded App Pro), with a custom-priced Organisation tier for enterprises.

faqs.question: Does Disciple take a cut of my revenue?
faqs.answer: No, Disciple applies a 0% transaction fee policy across all plans; customers keep the revenue they collect, subject to standard payment-processing fees.

faqs.question: Do I get a real iOS and Android app?
faqs.answer: Yes, every plan includes a fully branded native app listed in the Apple App Store and Google Play Store, plus a web version.

faqs.question: Who founded Disciple?
faqs.answer: Disciple was founded around 2013 by Benji Vaughan and Jeremy Smith in London.

faqs.question: Can I sell courses through Disciple?
faqs.answer: Yes, monetizable courses are available starting on the Branded App Plus plan and above.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Disciple Media and Disciple?
faqs.answer: Disciple is the current brand name for the platform formerly marketed as Disciple Media; disciplemedia.com now redirects to disciple.community.

use_cases.title: Creator and coaching membership communities
use_cases.description: Coaches and creators launch a branded app combining paid courses, group discussion, and livestreams for their audience.

use_cases.title: Brand and fan communities
use_cases.description: Brands and artists build a dedicated, ad-free space for fans and customers separate from mainstream social platforms.
